content_markdown:
A gas is in a vertical cylinder fitted with a piston. Weights are placed on the piston. When the gas is at $27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ the piston is in equilibrium at height $h$ above the base of the cylinder as shown below.

To what value should the gas temperature be increased for the piston to be in equilibrium at a height $2h$ above the base?

diagram_metadata.description: A vertical cylinder containing gas, with a piston on top supporting three weights. The height of the gas column is labeled as h.

vibe_explanation: Since the weights on the piston remain the same, the pressure $P$ is constant. According to Charles's Law ($V \propto T$), the volume is proportional to the absolute temperature in Kelvin. Doubling the height $h$ to $2h$ doubles the volume $V$. The initial temperature is $27\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 300\,\text{K}$. Doubling this gives $600\,\text{K}$. Converting back to Celsius: $600 - 273 = 327\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.
